---
title: "13 · Basic Probability"
subtitle: "Quantifying chance — favourable over total"
lesson-id: "t3.basic-probability"
tier: 3
prerequisites: ["t3.combinatorics-counting", "t1.fractions-as-parts"]
unlocks: ["t3.conditional-probability", "t3.discrete-distributions"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research", "databases"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [11 · Combinatorics](../11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)
   and [T1 L07 — Fractions](../../tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with $\binom{n}{r}$ and the multiplication principle?
3. Have you ever predicted "70% chance of rain" or "1 in 14 million
   to win the lottery"?
:::

## Touch

Pick a die-roll outcome. The widget shows the **probability** as a
fraction and a percentage.

```{ojs}
viewof event = Inputs.select(["roll a 6", "roll an even number", "roll greater than 4", "roll a 7", "roll any number 1–6"], {value: "roll a 6", label: "Event:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let favourable, total = 6;
  switch(event) {
    case "roll a 6": favourable = 1; break;
    case "roll an even number": favourable = 3; break; // 2, 4, 6
    case "roll greater than 4": favourable = 2; break; // 5, 6
    case "roll a 7": favourable = 0; break;
    case "roll any number 1–6": favourable = 6; break;
  }
  const p = favourable / total;
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.4rem;">
      P(event) = <span style="color:#c2185b; font-weight:bold;">${favourable}/${total}</span>
      = <span style="color:#1565c0; font-weight:bold;">${p.toFixed(3)}</span>
      = <span style="color:#2e7d32; font-weight:bold;">${(p * 100).toFixed(1)}%</span>
    </div>
    <div style="font-family:monospace; margin-top:0.4rem; color:#666;">
      ${favourable} favourable outcomes out of ${total} possible
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Probability of an event = (favourable outcomes) / (total possible
outcomes) — when each outcome is **equally likely**. Always between 0
(impossible) and 1 (certain).

## Wonder

1. P(roll a 6) = 1/6. What's P(*not* a 6)?
2. P(roll an even number) + P(roll an odd number) = ?
3. Roll *two* dice. What's P(sum = 7)? More or less than P(sum = 12)?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A bag contains 3 red and 2 blue balls. Pick one at random. What is
P(red)? P(blue)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
P(red) = 3/5 = 0.6. P(blue) = 2/5 = 0.4. Sum is 1 (one of the two
must occur).
</details>

### Problem 2

Roll two fair six-sided dice. What is the **sample space** (set of
all outcomes)? How big is it?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each die independently: 6 outcomes. Multiplication principle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\{(1,1), (1,2), \ldots, (6,6)\}$. **36 outcomes.** Each is equally
likely (1/36).

The sample space is "all possible outcomes." Probability is computed
relative to it.
</details>

### Problem 3

Two dice are rolled. What is P(sum = 7)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
List the favourable outcomes: $(1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2),
(6,1)$ — 6 outcomes.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
P(sum = 7) = 6/36 = 1/6 ≈ 0.167.

Sum 7 is the **most likely** sum. P(sum = 12) = 1/36 (only $(6,6)$).
P(sum = 2) = 1/36 (only $(1,1)$). The middle sums are favoured because
more pairs add to them.
</details>

### Problem 4

A standard 52-card deck. P(drawing a heart)? P(drawing an ace)?
P(drawing the ace of hearts)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
P(heart) = 13/52 = 1/4.
P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13.
P(ace of hearts) = 1/52.

Notice: P(ace AND heart) = 1/52 = P(ace) × P(heart) = $1/13 \cdot 1/4
= 1/52$. The two attributes (rank, suit) are *independent* (Lesson 14).
</details>

### Problem 5

Toss a fair coin 3 times. P(exactly 2 heads)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total outcomes: $2^3 = 8$. Favourable: HHT, HTH, THH — that's
$\binom{3}{2} = 3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
P(exactly 2 heads in 3 tosses) = $\binom{3}{2}/2^3 = 3/8 = 0.375$.

Combinatorics returns: $\binom{3}{2}$ counts the *which-toss-is-tails*
choices. This is the **binomial probability** with $n = 3, k = 2,
p = 1/2$ — formal in [Lesson 15](../15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd).
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Probability as a mathematical subject was *born in a letter exchange*.
In **1654**, the gambler **Antoine Gombaud, Chevalier de Méré**, asked
how to fairly divide the stakes in an interrupted game of chance.
**Blaise Pascal** wrote to **Pierre de Fermat** about it; their
six-letter correspondence that summer founded modern probability
theory. The "fair division of stakes" is now called the *problem of
points* — and the binomial coefficients you met in Lesson 12 fall out
of its solution.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Probability is just careful counting.** Lay out every outcome,
mark the favourable ones, and divide. Every dazzling formula in
this and the next two lessons is a re-packaging of that one
fraction.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **sample space** $S$ is the set of all possible outcomes of an
experiment. An **event** $E$ is a subset of $S$.

For an experiment with **equally likely outcomes**:
$$P(E) = \dfrac{|E|}{|S|} = \dfrac{\text{favourable}}{\text{total}}.$$

**Properties:**

- $0 \le P(E) \le 1$.
- $P(\emptyset) = 0$ (impossible event).
- $P(S) = 1$ (something happens).
- $P(\text{not } E) = 1 - P(E)$ (the **complement rule**).
- For two **mutually exclusive** events $E_1, E_2$ (no overlap):
  $P(E_1 \text{ or } E_2) = P(E_1) + P(E_2)$.
- For any two events: $P(E_1 \cup E_2) = P(E_1) + P(E_2) - P(E_1 \cap E_2)$
  (the **inclusion–exclusion principle**).

:::

> *Probability = favourable / total (for equally likely outcomes);*
> *Always between 0 and 1; sum over all outcomes is 1;*
> *Complement rule: $P(\text{not } E) = 1 - P(E)$.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A drawer holds 5 black socks and 3 white socks (no pairs sorted).
> You grab 2 at random. What is P(both same colour)?

A competent solver:

1. **Total ways to grab 2 from 8:** $\binom{8}{2} = 28$.
2. **Both black:** $\binom{5}{2} = 10$.
3. **Both white:** $\binom{3}{2} = 3$.
4. **Same colour total:** $10 + 3 = 13$.
5. **P(same colour):** $13/28 \approx 0.464$.

Or compute the *complement* — P(different colours) = $\binom{5}{1}
\binom{3}{1} / 28 = 15/28$ — and subtract: $1 - 15/28 = 13/28$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Roll a die. P(1 or 2)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
2/6 = 1/3.
</details>

**W2.** Bag with 4 red, 6 blue marbles. P(blue)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
6/10 = 3/5.
</details>

**W3.** P(*not* a heart from a deck)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 - 1/4 = 3/4$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Two dice. P(sum is 8 or 11)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sums are mutually exclusive. P(8) + P(11).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum 8: $(2,6),(3,5),(4,4),(5,3),(6,2)$ — 5 outcomes.
Sum 11: $(5,6),(6,5)$ — 2.
Total favourable: 7. P = 7/36.
</details>

**P2.** From a deck of 52 cards, draw 5 without replacement. P(all 5
are hearts)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total hands: $\binom{52}{5}$. Heart hands: $\binom{13}{5}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\binom{13}{5}}{\binom{52}{5}} = \dfrac{1287}{2598960} \approx
0.000495$ — about **1 in 2000**.
</details>

**P3.** A card is drawn from a deck. P(king OR red card)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Inclusion-exclusion: $P(K) + P(R) - P(K \text{ AND } R)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(K) = 4/52$. $P(R) = 26/52$. $P(K \cap R) = 2/52$ (red kings:
heart and diamond).
$P(K \cup R) = 4/52 + 26/52 - 2/52 = 28/52 = 7/13$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Birthday paradox.** In a room of 23 people, the probability
that *some pair shares a birthday* is over 50%. (Surprising!) Compute
P(no shared birthday) for $n$ people, then 1 minus that for shared.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$P(\text{no shared}) = \dfrac{365}{365} \cdot \dfrac{364}{365} \cdot
\dfrac{363}{365} \cdots \dfrac{365 - n + 1}{365}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For $n = 23$:
$P(\text{no shared}) = \prod_{k=0}^{22} \dfrac{365 - k}{365} \approx 0.4927$.
$P(\text{shared}) \approx 0.5073$ — over 50%.

The reason: it's not "your birthday matches someone's" but "some pair
matches" — and there are $\binom{23}{2} = 253$ pairs to check, each
with $\approx 1/365$ chance.

This is the **birthday attack** in cryptography: hash collisions
become likely much sooner than naïve intuition suggests, which is why
hash functions must produce *much* longer outputs than one might
think necessary.
</details>

**S2.** A spam filter classifies an email as spam if it contains
certain keywords. The classifier predicts probability of spam $p \in
[0, 1]$. What rule should it use to *decide*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Decide "spam" if $p > 0.5$, else "not spam"** — the simplest
threshold. (Adjusted higher if false-positive cost is large, lower if
false-negative cost is large.)

This is the heart of **classification in machine learning**: a model
outputs a probability; a threshold decides the label. The choice of
threshold (the **decision boundary**) trades off precision and
recall — a foundational topic in information retrieval and ML
evaluation.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Probabilities can exceed 1."** No — never. If you compute
   $P > 1$, recheck (probably double-counted).
2. **"Independent and mutually exclusive are the same."** Opposite!
   Mutually exclusive events *cannot both happen*; independent events
   can both happen, and the joint probability factors as $P(A) \cdot
   P(B)$.
3. **"Probability tells what *will* happen."** It tells the *long-
   run frequency*. A coin with P(H) = 0.5 may give 7 heads in 10
   tosses; only over very many tosses does it converge to 0.5.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **[Probability](/glossary.qmd#probability) of an event is favourable
   outcomes over total outcomes** — a [fraction](/glossary.qmd#fraction)
   pinned between 0 and 1, when the outcomes are equally likely.
2. **The [sample space](/glossary.qmd#sample-space) is the unsung
   hero.** Half of "hard" probability problems are easy the moment
   you list the outcomes — the other half are easier still using the
   complement, $P(\text{not } E) = 1 - P(E)$.
3. **Combinatorics is the engine room.** Whenever the numerator and
   denominator are both counts, the [combinations](/glossary.qmd#combination)
   and permutations from Lesson 11 do the real work.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you compute P(roll an odd number) on a die in your head?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute P(both heads in 2 tosses)?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answers: 3/6 = 1/2; 1/4.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[14 · Conditional Probability](../14-conditional-probability/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (birthday paradox) — central counter-
  intuitive result.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[11 · Combinatorics](../11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L07 — Fractions](../../tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[14 · Conditional Probability](../14-conditional-probability/index.qmd)** —
  what changes when extra info is known.
- **[15 · Discrete Distributions](../15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)** —
  probability functions on possible values.
- **Tier 5 — Statistics & Probability Theory** — the rigorous version.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Every classifier outputs probabilities.
Many models (Naive Bayes, logistic regression) directly model
probabilities. Calibration of predicted probabilities is a major
subfield.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Birthday paradox (S1) sets fundamental limits on
hash function size. Cryptographic security analyses are statements
about probabilities.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Query estimation often involves probabilistic
analyses (selectivity estimates, sampling, sketches like
HyperLogLog and Bloom filters).
:::
